coredata.dc:title: A shikimic acid derived carbon dots (SACNDs-FITC) for multi-modal detection and removal of Hg2+: Probe design, sensing performance, and applications in food analysis

coredata.dc:description: The first shikimic acid derived fluorescent carbon dots (SACNDs-FITC) for multi-modal detection and simultaneous removal of Hg2+ is revealed. The fluorescence of SACNDs-FITC centered at 520 nm can be selectively quenched by Hg2+, while the emission centered at 420 nm remains constant which can be used for self-calibration. Naked-eye distinguishable color change from yellow to colourless under daylight and from green to blue under UV light could be observed for SACNDs-FITC in the real-time detection of Hg2+. Novel approaches that combine smartphone automated color hue (RGB and HSV) readout for intelligent Hg2+ analysis was developed. Moreover, SACNDs-FITC could work as an effective absorbent for Hg2+ with an adsorption capacity of 19.24 mg/g. Mechanism studies revealed the success of the post-modification strategy and the crucial role of −NCSN- groups in detecting and adsorbing Hg2+. Most importantly, multi-modal analysis of Hg2+ in celery cabbage and apples was performed using this analytical platform.

head.ce:title: A shikimic acid derived carbon dots (SACNDs-FITC) for multi-modal detection and removal of Hg2+: Probe design, sensing performance, and applications in food analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The first shikimic acid derived fluorescent carbon dots (SACNDs-FITC) for multi-modal detection and simultaneous removal of Hg2+ is revealed. The fluorescence of SACNDs-FITC centered at 520 nm can be selectively quenched by Hg2+, while the emission centered at 420 nm remains constant which can be used for self-calibration. Naked-eye distinguishable color change from yellow to colourless under daylight and from green to blue under UV light could be observed for SACNDs-FITC in the real-time detection of Hg2+. Novel approaches that combine smartphone automated color hue (RGB and HSV) readout for intelligent Hg2+ analysis was developed. Moreover, SACNDs-FITC could work as an effective absorbent for Hg2+ with an adsorption capacity of 19.24 mg/g. Mechanism studies revealed the success of the post-modification strategy and the crucial role of −NCSN- groups in detecting and adsorbing Hg2+. Most importantly, multi-modal analysis of Hg2+ in celery cabbage and apples was performed using this analytical platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+ is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions that has always been a subject of global concern due to its detrimental effects on human health and the environment [1]. Human exposure to Hg2+ primarily occurs through the consumption of contaminated water and food. Once ingested, Hg2+ can disrupt cellular functions by binding to thiol groups in proteins and enzymes, leading to oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction [2]. This results in a variety of health issues, including neurological disorders such as tremors, cognitive impairment, and developmental disorders in children. Furthermore, Hg2+ exposure has been linked with cardiovascular diseases, immunological disorders, and kidney damage [3]. The environmental ramifications of Hg2+ pollution are equally concerning, as they tend to accumulate in the biological chain [4]. Hg2+ are released into the environment through natural processes such as volcanic eruptions and the weathering of rocks, but anthropogenic activities including coal combustion, waste incineration, and industrial processes significantly exacerbate this release [5]. Therefore, the detection and control of Hg2+ level, particularly in drinking water and food products, is critical for safeguarding public health [6].

To date, significant strides have been made in the development of methodologies that allow for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ [7]. Instrumental analysis methods, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), have been developed [7]. However, these instrumental analysis methods commonly require costly equipment and skilled operators, which hamper their on-site detection. Molecular techniques, including polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based assays, have also been adapted for Hg2+ detection [8]. These methods capitalize on the ability of Hg2+ to inhibit DNA amplification, offering a genetic approach to detecting Hg2+ with high specificity [9]. Nevertheless, these methods require careful optimization to avoid false positives and are not readily accessible for point-of-care or field testing. Moreover, probes, including nanomaterials and small organic molecules, have proved to be efficient tools for real-time, selective, and in-field detection of Hg2+ [10,11]. However, to the best of our knowledge, colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probes capable of detecting and simultaneously removing Hg2+ are rarely reported. Furthermore, considering availability and user-friendliness, naked-eye on-site detection combined with smartphone automated analysis is greatly desirable [12].

Shikimic acid (SA, (3R, 4S, 5R)-(−)-3, 4, 5-trihydroxy-1-cyclohexenecarboxylic acid) is a clean and sustainable natural product derived from Chinese star anise in China (Scheme 1 , Part A) [13]. The distinctive structure of shikimic acid makes it a promising precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs). However, ratiometric fluorescent CDs derived from SA have not yet been reported. As a new progress in our continuous works on the utilization of shikimic acid and exploring new analytical methods [14–16], herein, the first SA-based ratiometric CDs probe (SACNDs-FITC) for multi-modal detection and simultaneously removing Hg2+ was revealed (Scheme 1, Part B, Table S1). SACNDs-FITC was synthesized from shikimic acid, followed by a post-modification with FITC to establish ratiometric fluorescence and introduce the selective binding site for Hg2+ (Scheme 1, Part A). SACNDs-FITC exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity in real-time detection of Hg2+, with a naked-eye distinguishable color change from yellow to colorless under daylight, and from green to blue under UV light. Based on the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence responses to Hg2+, methods combined with smartphone automated color hue readout (RGB and HSV) for intelligent Hg2+ analysis have been developed (Scheme 1, Part B). Adsorption studies suggest that the adsorption process is predominantly governed by multilayer chemisorption, with an adsorption capacity of 19.24 mg/g. Moreover, obvious aggregation was observed for SACNDs-FITC after detection of Hg2+, which could facilitate the removal of Hg2+. Additionally, mechanism studies revealed the crucial role of −NCSN- groups in the detection and adsorption of mercury ions (Scheme 1, Part B). Most importantly, point-of-care testing for the quantitative visual detection of Hg2+ in food samples has been performed using this analytical platform (Scheme 1, Part B).

Fluorescent spectra were recorded at room temperature using an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Shimadzu IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer. The microstructure of the materials was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) employing a JEOL JEM-2100PLUS instrument. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument from the UK. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was collected with a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi instrument from the USA.

All the reagents utilized in the experiments were commercially available and used without further purification. Shikimicacid (SA) and ethanediamine (EDA) were used to prepare fluorescent carbon dots (SACNDs). Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was used for post-modification of SACNDs. Na2CO3, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, KNO3, Al2(SO4)3, Zn(NO3)2, NaF, BaCl2, KCl, CoCl2, LiCl, CaCl2, MgSO4, FeCl2, CuSO4, Cr(NO3)3, Cd(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, and Hg(NO3)2 were used for the preparation of stock solutions of corresponding ions.

The optimum reaction time, temperature, and reactant ratio for the preparation of SACNDs were studied with control experiments, and the results are displayed in Fig. S1. The hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of SACNDs from shikimic acid according to the following procedures. In detail, shikimic acid (0.4 g, 2.3 mmol) and ethylenediamine (1 g, 16.6 mmol, 1.1 mL) were added to a PTFE reactor containing 10 mL of pure water and sonicated for 1 min to form a clear solution. After being sealed well, the hydrothermal reactors were heated at 180 °C in an oven for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the liquid supernatant was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min to remove large particulate suspensions. The obtained liquid supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was placed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag to dialyze for 24 h. The resulting SACNDs were obtained as solid after vacuum drying for 24 h.

0.2 g of SACNDs were dispersed in a mixture of water and ethanol (10 mL each, with a volume ratio of water to ethanol of 1:1) in a reaction tube. Subsequently, 20 mg of FITC was added to the reaction mixture. The mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was evaporated to dryness and the residue was combined with silica gel powder for further purification. Pure SACNDs-FITC was obtained using column chromatography with petroleum ether and ethyl acetate (5:1) as the eluent (Fig. S2). The progress of purification was monitored using thin-layer chromatography (Fig. S2). After the column chromatography, the mixture was subjected to dialysis for 24 h using a 1000 Da dialysis bag. The solid SACNDs-FITC was obtained by vacuum-drying for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stock solution preparation and stability testing of SACNDs-FITC
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of SACNDs-FITC was prepared by dispersing 200 mg of SACNDs-FITC into 100 mL deionized water to form a homogeneous solution. Ion stock solutions were prepared with the corresponding chemicals provided in Section 2.1 with a concentration of 10 mM. The stock solution of Hg2+ was prepared with Hg(NO3)2 in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 3.4, 10 mM). For pH stability testing, 200 μL of SACNDs-FITC stock solution was diluted to 2000 μL using Tris-HCl buffer solutions with different pH values. After that the corresponding fluorescent spectra were recorded. In the photo-stability study, 200 μL of the SACNDs-FITC stock solution was diluted to 2000 μL with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.0, 10 mM) and exposed to continuous irradiation for 1 h under a 365 nm UV lamp. The corresponding fluorescent spectra were recorded every 5 min. Furthermore, the long-term stability of SACNDs-FITC was monitored by recording the fluorescent spectra every 5 days over a period of 35 days.

The selectivity study was conducted as follows: To a Tris-HCl buffer solution (1700 μL), 200 μL of probe stock solution and 100 μL of ion stock solution were added, resulting in a final ion concentration of 50 μM. After incubation at room temperature for 10 min, the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded. In the interference experiments, 200 μL of probe stock solution, 100 μL of ion stock solution, and 100 μL of Hg2+ stock solution were added to the Tris-HCl buffer solution (1600 μL). The mixture was well mixed and incubated at room temperature for 10 min before performing the UV–Vis and fluorescence tests. In the fluorescence titration with Hg2+, different concentrations of Hg2+ (0 μM to 200 μM) were incubated with SACNDs-FITC (0.20 mg/mL) at room temperature for 10 min, and the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded. Each experiment was repeated three times to minimize errors and ensure accuracy.

The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC were calculated using a quinine sulfate solution (0.05 M) as the reference. Upon excitation at 350 nm, the emission spectrum ranged from 380 nm to 620 nm was recorded. The calculations were performed according to the following formula

To further investigate the feasibility of SACNDs-FITC in real sample analysis, recovery rates in celery cabbage and apples were assessed. The celery cabbage (5 g) and apple (5 g) samples were crushed separately using mortar-pestle, and then mixed with 50 mL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7) [19]. After that, the sample was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane. The filtrate was subsequently analyzed using SACNDs-FITC. To create spiked samples, a defined quantity of Hg2+ was introduced into the pretreated samples, which were then analyzed using SACNDs-FITC. Images of SACNDs-FITC solutions in the absence and presence of Hg2+ were captured using a smartphone camera and analyzed with the ColorPicker App to derive the RGB and HSV values.

The adsorption and removal performance of SACNDs-FITC on Hg2+ was assessed using the following methods. To study the adsorption kinetics, 0.2 g SACNDs-FITC was added to 100 mL Hg2+ solution at an initial concentration of 5 mg/L, varying the incubation times. After shaking the mixture at room temperature for a certain period of time, the mixture was centrifuged. The remaining Hg2+ in the supernatant was measured to evaluate the adsorption kinetics. The adsorption capacity was examined by introducing 0.2 g SACNDs-FITC into Hg2+ solutions with different initial concentrations. The equilibrium adsorption capacity was computed using equation (1):

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design and structural characterizations of SACNDs-FITC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to construct SACNDs from shikimic acid, ethylenediamine was used as a nitrogen source. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images and dynamic light scattering (DLS) test of SACNDs are displayed in Fig. S3a–c. Clearly, SACNDs with a uniform particle size of approximately 1.7 nm have been successfully constructed from shikimic acid. Control experiments using SA as the sole reactant were performed to prepare the carbon dots, SACDs. As depicted in Fig. S3d–e, similar particle size and fluorescence could observe for SACNDs compared with that of SACDs, indicating the dominant role of SA in the carbon dots' synthesis.

After the successful construction of SACNDs, post-modification was performed. FITC was employed as the targeting unit for modifying SACNDs based on the efficient reaction between amino groups and isothiocyanate. Following post-modification, new SACNDs-FITC were obtained. The HR-TEM images of SACNDs-FITC are displayed in Fig. 1 a–b. According to the HR-TEM analysis, the particle size of SACNDs-FITC is approximately 1.8 nm, which is consistent with the results of DLS testing (Fig. 1c). Most importantly, the post-modification introduces a new fluorescent emission, enabling the creation of ratiometric fluorescence without changing the structure of the SACNDs. Moreover, according to the XRD patterns of SACNDs (Fig. 1d) and SACNDs-FITC (Fig. 1e), there are broad peaks ranging from 10° to 40°, indicating that the structure of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC is primarily amorphous. It is worth noting that a peak attributed to the graphitic carbon nitride plane at around 27° can be observed in the PXRD pattern of SACNDs (Fig. 1d). However, the main peak shifted from 27° to 23° after post-modification [25,26]. This shift may be due to the large amount of FITC combining with the amino groups on the carbon dots, leading to the masking of the diffraction of the graphitic carbon nitride plane. Additionally, the zeta potential of SACNDs changed from −17.4 mV to −11.5 mV after reacting with FITC (Fig. 1f), indicating that FITC has successfully bonded with the amino groups [27].

The FT-IR spectra of SA, FITC, SACNDs, and SACNDs-FITC are displayed in Fig. 1g. The peak at 3480 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration, which is present in the structures of SA, FITC, SACNDs, and SACNDs-FITC. Both SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC display a characteristic N–H stretching vibration peak around 3400 cm−1. The characteristic stretching vibration of −N = C = S at 2050 cm−1 is observed in the FT-IR spectrum of FITC. However, this vibration disappears in the FT-IR spectrum of SACNDs-FITC, suggesting that the isothiocyanate has successfully bonding with amino groups. In comparison with SACNDs, a new peak at 1107 cm−1 is observed in the FT-IR spectrum of SACNDs-FITC, which is attributed to the vibration of CS. This peak further confirms the successful modification of FITC onto the surface of SACNDs [28].

To further analyze the surface elemental composition, the XPS spectra of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC were recorded. As demonstrated in Fig. 1h, the full XPS spectra of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC consist of three typical peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.5 eV, and 531.0 eV, respectively, and are ascribed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. Therefore, SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC are primarily composed of C, N, and O. However, in comparison to SACNDs, the XPS spectrum of SACNDs-FITC exhibits the typical S 2p signal at 162.0 eV [29] (Fig. 1i). This finding confirms the successful modification of FITC on the surface of SACNDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s in SACNDs (Fig. 1j) exhibits four constituent peaks at 284.6 eV (sp2C), 285.3 eV (C-N), 286.5 eV (C-O/C-OH), and 289.2 eV (CO). A similar constitution of C 1s is observed in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of SACNDs-FITC (Fig. 1j), except for a peak at 289.2 eV, which is attributed to CO/CS [30]. There are three constituent peaks in the N 1s band of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC (Fig. 1k). The N 1s peak in SACNDs is resolved into pyridine N at 398.7 eV, amino N at 399.5 eV, and graphitic N at 400.8 eV, while in SACNDs-FITC, it is ascribed to pyridine N at 398.7 eV, ArNHCSNHAr N at 400.4 eV, and graphitic N at 401.0 eV. Moreover, the O 1s exhibits two similar characteristic peaks in both SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC (Fig. 1l), corresponding to CO at 531.6 eV and C-O at 532.6 eV [31]. The XPS analysis results indicate that the exposed amino groups of SACNDs almost completely reacted with FITC, resulting in the formation of new chemical bonds represented as −Ar-NH-CS-NH-Ar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC
ce:section.ce:para: After determining the structure, the luminescent properties of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC were characterized. Initially, the three-dimensional excitation-emission contour maps for SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC were recorded and are displayed in Fig. S4. The corresponding two-dimensional excitation-emission contour maps are shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b. Clearly, there is only one contour peak for SACNDs (Fig. 2a). As expected, an additional new contour peak can be observed for SACNDs-FITC (Fig. 2b), which can be ascribed to the bonded FITC. The UV–Vis spectra of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC further confirm the above results. As shown in Fig. 2c–d, SACNDs have an absorption peak around 335 nm (Fig. 2c), while SACNDs-FITC has two absorption peaks at 338 nm and 494 nm (Fig. 2d), corresponding to SACNDs and FITC, respectively. It is evident from the above spectra tests that the optimum excitation wavelength for SACNDs is 340 nm, with an emission centered at 420 nm. For SACNDs-FITC, an excitation wavelength of 340 nm is optimal, resulting in two emission peaks, one around 420 nm and another around 520 nm.

Time-resolved emission-decay curves of SACNDs, FITC, and SACNDs-FITC were recorded and are depicted in Fig. 2e and 2f. According to Fig. 2e, the fluorescence lifetimes of SACNDs (λex = 420 nm) and FITC (λem = 520 nm) are 2.973 ns and 3.702 ns, respectively. After chemical modification with FITC, the fluorescence lifetime of SACNDs remained relatively unchanged at 3.015 ns (Fig. 2f), while the fluorescence lifetime corresponding to the emission of FITC in SACNDs-FITC was notably increased to 4.402 ns (Fig. 2f). This increase indicates a significant fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from SACNDs to FITC [32]. Moreover, using a 0.05 M quinine sulfate solution as a reference standard, the fluorescence quantum yields of SACNDs and SACNDs-FITC are determined to be 52.64 % and 57.85 %, respectively.

The fluorescent emission of SACNDs-FITC (λex = 340 nm, λem = 420 nm and 520 nm) in Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM) with different pH values was recorded. As can be seen from Fig. 2g, the intensity centered at 420 nm remains essentially stable in Tris-HCl buffer solution across a wide pH range. And the intensity centered at 520 is significantly higher and stable in buffer solutions with pH ranged from 3 to 10. Therefore, all fluorescent detection was carried out in Tris-HCl buffer solutions with a pH value of 7.0. Moreover, the fluorescent intensities of SACNDs-FITC after exposure to 365 nm UV light for various durations were recorded to reveal the photo-stability of SACNDs-FITC. As depicted in Fig. 2h, the fluorescent emissions of SACNDs-FITC maintain consistency over an hour of continuous UV exposure, indicating its good photo-stability. Additionally, the fluorescent intensities of SACNDs-FITC were recorded once every 5 days for 35 days. According to Fig. 2i, there is no significant change in fluorescence intensity for SACNDs-FITC over 35 days, suggesting its good stability [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The colorimetric and ratiometric selective detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: With the above results in hand, the colorimetric and ratiometric selective detection of Hg2+ were performed. Initially, the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of SACNDs-FITC in the presence of different ions were recorded. According to Fig. S5a, the addition of Hg2+ to SACNDs-FITC leads to a significant decrease in the absorption of SACNDs-FITC, while the addition of other ions causes no noticeable change. Similarly, the fluorescence emission of SACNDs-FITC centered at 520 nm significantly decreased after treatment with Hg2+ (Fig. S5b). It is worth mentioning that the fluorescence emission of SACNDs-FITC at 420 nm remain unchanged during the Hg2+ detection, which could be used for self-calibration and to provide ratiometric fluorescence. Therefore, the fluorescent intensity ratio of F520/F420 was used as an indicator in the fluorescent detection of Hg2+.

The corresponding absorbance and the fluorescent intensity ratio F520/F420 of SACNDs-FITC in the presence and absence of various analytes (50 μM) are shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b. Evidently, among the 19 ions screened, only Hg2+ notably affects the fluorescence and absorbance of SACNDs-FITC. This demonstrates the high selectivity of SACNDs-FITC in the detection of Hg2+. The interference experiments were conducted by recording the fluorescent intensity ratio F520/F420 and the absorbance (λ = 494 nm) of SACNDs-FITC in the presence of Hg2+ (50 μM) along with other analytes (50 μM). As depicted in Fig. 3c and d, the colorimetric and fluorescent responses of SACNDs-FITC to Hg2+ are not influenced by other ions which is evidenced by the unchanged absorbance intensity and fluorescent intensity ratio F520/F420 of SACNDs-FITC. Therefore, SACNDs-FITC exhibits good anti-interference ability in the colorimetric and ratiometric detection of Hg2+.

In order to further reveal the quantitative colorimetric and ratiometric responses of SACNDs-FITC to Hg2+, titration experiments were performed. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of SACNDs-FITC following treatment with 0–200 μM Hg2+ are recorded in Fig. 3e. Clearly, the absorbance of SACNDs-FITC gradually decreased with the increasing of Hg2+ concentration. Accompanying the above process, a noticeable color change from yellow-green to colorless could be observed with the naked eye (Insets in Fig. 3e). A linear fitting analysis between the absorbance intensity (λ = 494 nm) and Hg2+ concentration (1–10 μM, Fig. S5c) is depicted in Fig. 3f. A detection limit of 979.2 nM was determined using the linear fitting's slope (k) and the standard deviation (σ) with the formula 3σ/k. The fluorescent titration results are depicted in Fig. 3g, from which it can be observed that the fluorescence intensity centered at 520 nm gradually decreases, while the emission peak centered at 420 nm remains almost unchanged. The linear fitting analysis between the fluorescent intensity ratio F520/F420 and Hg2+ concentration (1–10 μM, Fig. S5d) is depicted in Fig. 3h. A detection limit of 56.4 nM was determined using the same method as described above (Fig. 3f). Moreover, the linear fitting analysis between the fluorescent intensity at 520 nm and Hg2+ concentration is displayed in Fig. S5e. A detection limit of 15.6 nM was determined according to the above linear fitting analysis. Additionally, the changes in CIE coordinates of SACNDs-FITC following treatment with different concentrations of Hg2+ have been plotted on the CIE 1931 diagram (Fig. 3i), which further confirms the chroma change during the Hg2+ titration [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative, visual and intelligent detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the significant color changes (Fig. 3e) and the remarkable fluorescence chromaticity coordinates shifts of SACNDs-FITC following treatment with Hg2+(Fig. 3i), a novel approach combining smartphone-automated color hue readout for intelligent Hg2+ analysis has been developed. A schematic diagram of the SACNDs-FITC-based multi-modal detection of Hg2+ is displayed in Fig. 4 a-4d. The RGB and HSV values of SACNDs-FITC solutions with different concentration Hg2+ were analyzed with the ColorPicker App (Fig. 4c-4d). The corresponding RGB and HSV values were listed in Table S2 [35]. As can be seen from Fig. 4c, a distinguishable color fading from yellow to colorless with the gradual increase in Hg2+ concentration could be observed by the naked eye. The corresponding HSV values (Hue) correlated well with the Hg2+ concentration, with a good correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9986). A detection limit of 99.3 nM was obtained by using the above linear fitting according to the formula LOD = 3σ/K, σ is standard deviation of blank (the detailed process for the calculation of σ See supporting information), K is calibrate slope of the curve (Fig. 4c) [18,36]. Moreover, the fluorescence of SACNDs-FITC changed from yellow-green to blue accompanying the aforementioned process. The changes in the Red-Green-Blue (RGB) color values, particularly the ratio of green to blue (G/B), demonstrate a strong linear correlation with Hg2+ concentration, as indicated by a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9932). A detection limit of 350.6 nM was determined using the same method (Fig. 4d). Additionally, semi-quantitative determination using the naked eye was performed by individual volunteers, as presented with good accuracy in Fig. S6 [37].

To assess the practicality of SACNDs-FITC for detecting Hg2+ in real samples, a spiked recovery approach was employed. The samples examined included celery cabbage and apples each of which was subjected to the pre-treatment protocols outlined in the experimental section (Section 2.6). Concentrations of Hg2+ in the above samples were determined using the corresponding linear calibration equations depicted in Fig. 3f, Fig. 3h, and Fig. 4c–d. The corresponding results were summarized in Table 1 . It is observed that no detectable Hg2+ was present in the blank samples. The recovery rates were calculated using the formula: Recovery (%) = C1/C0 × 100 %. (In this formula, C1 represents the concentration of Hg2+ as determined by the calibration curve, while C0 is the known concentration of Hg2+ added to the samples) [38]. The calculated recovery rates are presented in Table 1. The recoveries for Hg2+ in celery cabbage and apple samples are in the range of 99.5 % to 101.8 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of less than 4.5 %. These findings attest to the high sensitivity and practical applicability of SACNDs-FITC in detecting trace amounts of Hg2+ in food samples [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and removal of Hg2+ in water
ce:section.ce:para: Further studies were undertaken to evaluate the adsorption and removal efficiency of SACNDs-FITC in relation to Hg2+. Initially, the adsorption kinetics of SACNDs-FITC for Hg2+ was scrutinized to gauge the influence of contact duration. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the adsorption rate increased rapidly initially, which is attributed to the ample adsorption sites available on SACNDs-FITC and the opposite charges on the surface of SACNDs-FITC and Hg2+. The coordination binding of Hg2+ with −NCSN- requires more time, after which the adsorption rate gradually decreased. The system reaches a dynamic equilibrium after approximately 120 min. To gain a more profound insight into the adsorption kinetics, both the pseudo-first-order (PFO, Equation 3) and pseudo-second-order (PSO, Equation 4) kinetic models were applied for analysis [40].The pseudo-second-order and pseudo-first-order kinetic model fittings for Hg2+ adsorption onto SACNDs-FITC are shown in Fig. 5b and 5c, respectively. The corresponding kinetic parameters are delineated in Table 2 . The PSO model provided a superior fit to the adsorption kinetics of SACNDs-FITC for Hg2+, as evidenced by a higher correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9803). This suggests that the adsorption process is predominantly governed by chemisorption, indicating a chemical interaction between Hg2+ and the binding sites on SACNDs-FITC.

The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were employed to analyze the adsorption mechanism. The influence of Hg2+ concentration was scrutinized through the analysis of the adsorption isotherm for SACNDs-FITC, as shown in Fig. 5d. The experimental data were then fitted using both the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, with the results shown in Fig. 5e-5f. A detailed table of the adsorption parameters is provided in Table 2. Notably, based on these parameters, the Freundlich model exhibits a higher correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9722) than the Langmuir model, suggesting that the adsorption of Hg2+ onto SACNDs-FITC is better described by the Freundlich model, which is characteristic of multilayer adsorption [41]. Furthermore, the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of SACNDs-FITC for Hg2+ was determined to be 19.24 mg/g, indicating a moderate adsorption capacity (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing and adsorption mechanism study
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the detection and removal mechanism, Zeta potential of the SACNDs-FITC solution before and after being treated with Hg2+ was recorded. According to Fig. 6 a, the initial Zeta potential of SACNDs-FITC is −11.5 mV, and it changed to +3.4 mV after the introduction of Hg2+, suggesting that the positively charged Hg2+ have been bound to the surface of SACNDs-FITC. The particle size of SACNDs-FITC after being treated with Hg2+ was studied using a DLS test. As shown in Fig. 6b, remarkable aggregation is observed for SACNDs-FITC after the detection of Hg2+ when compared with the fresh SACNDs-FITC (Fig. 1c). This aggregation can be attributed to the reduced surface potential of the particles and the role of Hg2+ as a linker between SACNDs-FITC particles.

The time-resolved emission-decay curves of SACNDs-FITC after treated with Hg2+ were recorded. According to Fig. 6c, the fluorescence lifetime of SACNDs-FITC are 3.003 ns (λem = 420 nm) and 3.527 ns (λem = 520 nm), respectively. Comparatively, the fluorescence lifetime of emission at 420 nm did not change significantly (Fig. 2f, 3.015 ns vs Fig. 6c, 3.003 ns). However, the fluorescence lifetime of the emission at 520 nm was notably decreased (Fig. 2f, 4.402 ns vs Fig. 6c, 3.527 ns). Therefore, there might be the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between SACNDs-FITC and Hg2+ which would cause the quenching of the emission at 520 nm [42].

In order to study the detailed interaction between Hg2+ and SACNDs-FITC, XPS tests were performed on the samples that after detecting Hg2+. As can be seen from Fig. 6d, the peaks of C 1s and O 1s are located at 284.8 eV and 531.3 eV, respectively. The binding energies of these two elements have remained almost unchanged compared with those in the fresh SACNDs-FITC (Fig. 1h). However, a new peak at 101.0 eV corresponding to Hg 4f appeared in the full XPS spectrum of SACNDs-FITC that after detecting Hg2+ (Fig. 6d), which further confirms the combination between Hg2+ and SACNDs-FITC. Similar peak constitutions of C 1s are observed in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of SACNDs-FITC before and after being treated with Hg2+ (Fig. 1j vs. Fig. 6e) [43]. A new peak corresponding to nitrate is observed at 530.3 eV in the high-resolution spectra of O 1s after being treated with Hg2+ [44] (Fig. 6f). Interestingly, the N1s peak is located at 398.3 eV in SACNDs-FITC after detection of Hg2+ (Fig. 6g), and a decrease in the N 1s binding energy is observed (from 400.6 eV to 398.3 eV, Fig. 1h vs. Fig. 6d). This may be attributed to the formation of a chemical bond between Hg2+ and N atoms, as illustrated by the high-resolution spectra of Hg-N (Fig. 6g, 397.5 eV) [45].

According to Fig. S7, there is no obvious overlap between the absorbance spectrum of Hg2+ and the excitation/emission spectra of SACNDs-FITC. Therefore, the inner filter effect (IFE) can be excluded as a cause for the fluorescent quenching observed at 520 nm [47]. Based on the findings presented, it is evident that Hg2+ can bind to the surface of SACNDs-FITC with –NCSN– groups and form new bonds including Hg-N and Hg-S. According to the fluorescence lifetime changes and the XPS analysis, electron transfer from S to Hg2+ could be confirmed [48]. Therefore, the PET effect from the FITC sub-unit to Hg2+ could be responsible for the fluorescent quenching of the emission at 520 nm (Fig. 6j). Moreover, due to the significant reduction in surface potential and the close association of SACNDs-FITC particles through Hg2+ linkages, obvious aggregation was observed for SACNDs-FITC following Hg2+ detection (Fig. 6j). Accordingly, the formation of SACNDs-FITC aggregation would facilitate subsequent centrifugal separation of Hg2+ (Fig. 6j).

In conclusion, SACNDs-FITC for sensitive detection and highly efficient removal of Hg2+ has been first developed through a post-modification strategy from shikimic acid. SACNDs-FITC exhibits selective colorimetric and ratiometric responses to Hg2+ with a naked-eye distinguishable color change from yellow to colorless under daylight and from green to blue under UV light. Based on the linear changes in HSV (Saturation values) and RGB (G/B ratio) of SACNDs-FITC solution with different concentrations of Hg2+, novel approaches that combine smartphone-automated color hue readout for intelligent Hg2+ analysis have been developed. The detection limits for colorimetric, fluorescence, ratiometric, RGB and HSV modal are 979.2 nM, 15.6 nM, 56.4 nM, 350.6 nM and 99.3 nM, respectively. Adsorption experiments revealed that the adsorption process is predominantly governed by multilayer chemisorption, with an adsorption capacity of 19.24 mg/g. Moreover, mechanism studies indicated that the –NCSN– group introduced by the post-modification strategy played a crucial role in the detection and removal of mercury ions. Additionally, this multi-modal detection method has been successfully applied to the analysis of Hg2+ in cabbage and apples.
